caption.text: Biking 🚴 Across America 🇺🇸 Recap. 2,632 Miles in 26 days of biking - over 100+ miles per day. Washington D.C. to Seattle, WA. 15 states. Lots of highs and lows physically and literally. Learned a lot about America, small towns, my preferences for a city and culture, the wind, bikes and cycling in general, Amish people, Native Americans, and so much more. I met amazing people, encountered difficulties I wouldn’t have expected, and pushed my limits. I am so grateful for everyone that supported me along the way. Raised thousands and counting fighting heart 🫀 disease and obesity and hopefully have inspired some people along the way to take some risks and try something unknown. Posted a recap of some lessons I learned on LinkedIn if you search my name. Thank you all for the support ❤️👍

caption.text: The final day, day 27, complete. Space needle reached. Mission accomplished. 106 miles from just outside Ellensberg, WA to the Space Needle in Seattle, WA. The final total is 2,632 miles. I will break down the trip and offer some concluding thoughts in a post in the next day or so. Today was an adventure. Some incline but lots of decline as I came back to sea level. Went through a 2.6 mile pitch black tunnel, called the Snoqualmie tunnel. My light died about .5 miles in so I am thankful to my phone and its flashlight which allowed me to keep moving. I soon moved into Seattle suburbs and coasted on trails all the way into the city over some beautiful bridges. Saw some fighter jets in the sky. Biked through the city to the Space Needle where I met my parents, took some photos, and went to the top. Glad to be finished. Thank you everyone for the support 🙏. Breakdown coming soon 👍

caption.text: Day 26 complete. 66 miles in Washington brining the total to 2,526. I have entered and rode in my 15th and final state today. Rode from Spokane down to Ellensberg. Spokane is shockingly populated by homeless people. Saw one woman who was alone, crying and screaming this morning. She was saying “get off of me” and “I need a knife.” She was less than 10 years older than me. So sad to see human life reduced to that. Washington’s terrain was not what I expected. Lots of rolling hills, wheat and grassland. A lot of the trails I was intending to ride were emergency closed due to fires and the interstate was packed so had to skip over some sections. Temp was back hot again at 90 F but the headwind wasn’t too bad at 11. Final day tmr. Excited for the culmination of the trip. 👍

caption.text: Day 25 complete. 74 miles from outside Missoula, MT to outside Spokane, WA bringing the total to 2,460. Woke up to some heavy rain which fortunately wiped the smoke out. Went to Adventure Cycle in the am in Missoula and got my photo taken on a wall which has a bunch of other people coming through town doing big bike trips. Avoided some dicey interstate riding early on and spent the day on the Coeur d’Alene trail, traversing the Idaho panhandle. Got drenched early on but then it cleared up and became spectacularly beautiful. Paved trail and great terrain made for some great riding. Legs are definitely dusted after the last few days. Nearing completion and, if all goes well, should reach the Space Needle in two days. Now in Pacific Coast time. 👍

caption.text: Day 24 complete. 108 miles from Butte to just outside Missoula. Total 2,386 miles on 23 days of riding. Another rigorous day. Woke up and got going in the am and it was cold, around 48 F in the am. As I got moving it was nice however the smoke is still very bad, hoping to be past it tomorrow. Around 1pm the wind picked up to 30mph+ again and at some points going up hill, I felt that I would have been going faster if I was walking. Saw a cool old Montana prison in Deerlodge and waded into the Clarkfork near the end of the day which was very relaxing. Getting closer to the finish line. 👍

caption.text: Day 23 complete. Back at it with 109 miles from outside Bozeman, MT to Butte, America. Total 2,278. Heat definitely cooled off as temps were in the low-high 70s F today. Smoke, however, got even worse. Felt like I was in a post-apocalyptic world at some points. Made decent speed today, however, from 2-530pm I faced a 30mph headwind and steep uphill as I neared Butte. Was in national forest territory so was largely empty and half expected bears to emerge but lots of terrain had just been wiped out by fires. Despite distance and smoke obscuring my view, it was still cool to see the famous Our Lady of the Rockies statue. Fun fact: Though now largely a shadow of its past, in the late 1800s, coinciding with the the push to electrify America with Copper, Butte became largely overnight “The Richest Hill on Earth,” the most lucrative copper mining region in the world. Lot’s of history 👍

caption.text: Day 22 complete. Hot and Smokey. Up to 100 F temps and smoke from northwestern Montana fires made today rigorous but kept moving. Around 126 miles from Red Lodge to just near Bozeman, MT. Totaling 2,169. Lots of back country and frontage roads. Heading home for a few days and will service the bike (wheels fine but need to fix front tire) and get ready for the final couple hundred miles. Parents have a wedding to go to so and some other things that need to be taken care of so will wait for them so they can join me on the last leg as well. Don’t exactly want to take a break but this will enable me to finish the last stretch with family while also allowing temps to cool and smoke to hopefully dissipate. Back again in a few days 👍 (Expected Sunday 7/28)

caption.text: Day 21 complete. 127 miles from Sheridan, WY to Lovell, WY. Totaling 2,043. Wild day. Around 10,000 feet of elevation gain and decline on the “Oh My God” pass. The climb was rigorous but the views were amazing. Decline was so beautiful and hit 40+mph speeds. Saw a moose and its baby, saw snow at the peak, and experienced temperatures from 50 all the way to 95 F. Escaped some thunder in the afternoon. Bouncing up to Red Lodge, MT tonight. 👍

caption.text: Day 20 complete. 85 miles from Custer, SD to Moorcroft, WY. Totaling 1,916. Biking close to Black Hills National Forrest in the morning was beautiful. Then once I got into Wyoming the terrain mostly just became a barren, but beautiful, expanse. A bit of rain which was nice and thunder in the afternoons but avoided anything heavy. Temp was cool in the morning with the fresh mountain air. Headwind was pretty constant around 14mph. Expecting tall mountains tmr 👍

caption.text: Day 19 complete. 60 miles from Chadron, NE to short of Custer, SD. Forgot to mention I entered Mountain standard time yesterday. Got a late start as I spent the morning putting on the new rear wheel. Had more difficulties with my pump and the valve core so eventually got started just past noon. Afternoon started off very windy (at one point I was biking fully leaning sideways into the wind) and hot (99 F) but then the weather took a turn and severe thunder and lightning rolled in. Entered a new state (SD) but had to call it not long after due to the weather. Terrain is beautiful. Had Elk bolognese and Bison burger for dinner with my dad, those proteins are how I know I am getting closer to Montana. Totaling  1,831 👍

caption.text: Day 18 complete. Hot, humid, high wind. 82 miles bringing us to 1,771. Valentine, NE to just shy of Gordon, NE. Got started a bit late and was a bit fatigued from yesterday. The wind got started a bit late too but was not fatigued as it gave me a 20mph headwind for most of the day, felt like I was biking into a wall. Rode through the middle of nowhere in the Sandhills, saw a lot of cows, and met with my dad and dog at the end of the day. Will hopefully get the wheel fixed tomorrow, very glad it held up today. 👍

accessibility_caption: Photo by John Chadwell on July 17, 2024.

caption.text:
Big updates:

First, primarily due to the heat (130 degrees in Death Valley), I am switching my route to the northern one I outlined earlier with Seattle as my final destination. This will also enable me to further follow the Great American Rail Trail, go through my home state Montana, and be closer to family. The route is slightly longer than going to LA, however, it should only be a few hundred miles more. An approximation of the route is included above.

Secondly, the second half of this journey will no longer be completely solo. Nine of the last ten days I have either had breakage or repairs done to my bike. Despite expert assurances that the bike would hold my weight, it has not. I take full responsibility for not doing more thorough research into a better bike that could sustain my weight and necessary equipment. Despite shedding weight, the bike continues to falter, namely through spoke breakage. Recognizing that there is not a legitimate bike shop between Norfolk, NE and Bozeman, MT, and that I am sitting back with two broken spokes without the necessary equipment to fix them, I need a solution before entering stretches in Wyoming where I will go for hours before seeing a town, other car, gas station, or have cell service. This solution is a new rear wheel to sustain extra weight combined with me shedding all weight aside from myself. Fortunately, my dad and dog Theo are able to come down from Montana and join me at least till Montana. My dad is bringing the new wheel and will accompany me on this next leg. I will still ride the vast majority of miles solo, however my dad will likely do a handful of them here and there which I won’t count towards my total. I expect to meet him tomorrow at some point along my day’s ride. Despite not continuing the journey solo, I am very excited for the next leg and for my dad and dog to join me. Great day today and back at it tmr (if the bike holds) 👍

Day 17 complete. Legs are gone. 160 miles from Neligh to Valentine, NE. Big updates on a subsequent post. Breakage continues. Left Neligh just about at 8am after some milk, yogurt, bread, and peanut butter. Day started off a bit rough with a 21mph headwind in my face and after about 1hr I broke another spoke in my rear wheel. I pushed on and eventually the head wind shifted into a tail wind. Felt like I was riding the Tour de France with the reduced weight and emptyish roads. Had a bit of a detour but saved a lot of extra miles thanks to advice from @ryanh_fung, one of the bikers I met a few days earlier going the opposite way. I stopped for lunch briefly at a conoco on the way as I wanted to hit a big number today. Jacob at the counter was making popcorn and told me it was the best popcorn I’ll ever have. I didn’t take the bait but then he gave me some for free after I got some water and milk. Unfortunately it was not the best popcorn I’ve ever had but he was a very nice guy. That was the only major stop. Sandhills were beautiful and it was a nice and cool day. Unfortunately broke a second spoke in the last 20 miles but was able to keep riding because the wheel held decently steady. Total miles 1,689👍

caption.text: Day 16 complete. Back on the road. Norfolk to Neligh, NE. Woke up, went to UPS, and shipped 12.5 pounds home to try and avoid more broken spokes and make myself faster. Then walked my bike 2 miles to the Norfolk bike shop where I dropped off my bike and then went to get breakfast in the town. After having breakfast with an elderly couple who were Norfolks “citizens of the year,” I returned to the bike shop and talked with the owner Jason and learned more about bike repair. Jason fixed my broken spokes, replaced my chain, realigned part of my derailer, and trued the rim. Grateful for him squeezing me in 🙏. Really nice guy and he got me back on the road around 3:15 so I decided to get a few miles in on Nebraska’s cowboy trail. 40 miles later I’m in Neligh sitting at 1,529 total. 👍

caption.text: Day 15 complete(ish). Woke up in Denison, Iowa ready to go but my equipment wasn’t. Valve core on my front tire came out of the tubeless set up as I was filling it up so I had to problem shoot and use pliers to screw it back in and fill the tire up. This set me back about an hour. Then I got moving, it was hot, around 95 again, but it was pretty and I was moving fast. I passed over the Missouri and had two chicken sandwiches and a burger at a Veteran place called Broadway Brothers in Decatur, Nebraska. I got moving not too long after but about 15 minutes into riding, as I was going down a hill (lots of rolling hills in NE), a second spoke broke on my rear wheel and the wheel started wobbling, rendering the bike unridable. On the side of the road in the middle of nowhere I tried to hitchhike but having no luck, I called an auto body shop, Mid America Enterprise, around 10 miles away and the owner, John Sachs, came and picked me up, brought me to the shop and gave me a cold water. Soon after, he, his wife Nicole and I set off for Norfolk, NE (the nearest town with a bike shop) where they dropped me off. The owners wife’s sister heard about what I was doing and wanted to donate $100 to the cause in memory of their brother, Jake Morris who sadly committed suicide. I am thankful for them 🙏. Now in Norfolk, I have to wait till tomorrow when the only bike shop in town opens at 12pm and hope they can squeeze me in. I will also ship some weight home. Disappointing but I expected this somewhere along the route. I completed 72 miles before the setback bringing us to 1,489 👍

caption.text: Day 14 complete. 105 miles from Ames to Denison, IA. Brings us to 1,417. Iowa refuses to let up. Broke another spoke on the rear wheel 2 minutes into the day. Had to keep going straight into a 40mph cross wind which later became a 30mph headwind. 96 deg Fahrenheit today as well. Saw the Missouri Mississippi divide which was cool around mile 80 and then it got cooler/wind went down and was able to go much faster. Not sure when I’ll fix the spike, hopefully soon. Met some cool guys in Jefferson, IA biking from Seattle to Boston doing around 70m a day. 👍

caption.text: Day 13 complete. Another rigorous day. Iowa has not been easy. 116 miles basically in a 37 mph cross wind the whole time. Cedar Rapids to Ames, IA. Stopped for lunch in Marshallstown but otherwise just gritted out miles 8am-7pm. Shoulder was operational this am despite rest of body being exhausted so we keep moving. Staying at an old fraternity house on Iowa State’s university campus tonight. Total 1,312 thus far. Stay hard. 👍

caption.text: DAY 12 COMPLETE. 122 miles bringing us to 1,196. Rigorous day. Had brunch in Fulton, Illinois and met a nice old motor biker guy. Then crossed the Mississippi which was cool. Soon after Iowa emerged, replete with hills, lots of bugs attacking me, dogs charging me, sun beaming on me, and deep gravel roads. Took my first fall of the trip in some gravel and bruised my shoulder a good bit and cut my leg. Should be sore tmr but good to go. Went from Rock Falls, IL to Cedar Falls, IA. Pretty sunset here and a nice town 👍

caption.text: Day 11 complete. 113 miles from Chicago to Rock Falls, IL. Brining us to 1,074 total. Woke up, had a good breakfast and hit the road, trails at the beginning and a bit of rain but then sun and country roads the rest of the day. Didn’t see too many people but saw a lot of wind turbines and corn. 👍

caption.text: Day 10, rest and recovery day. Woke up with a bit of rain left from Beryl, tested the bike, and decided it needed some repair. Took it to a bike shop and they were fortunately able to squeeze me in. Replaced the broken spoke in the back but had to go with tubed tires instead of my previous tubeless setup. Also lubricated the gears. Feels much better and ready to go. Ate lots of food today and relaxed the body. Was nice to stay with family. Still sitting at 961 miles, back at it tmr. 👍

caption.text: Day 9 complete. 113 miles today racing hurricane Beryl and tornado warnings in Indiana. Woke up to a broken spoke in my back wheel (not sure when it happened). Had to beat the hurricane and tornado path so biked on. Got absolutely drenched but made good time. Biked through mostly cornfields, some bike trails, small towns, and Chicago suburbs. It’s unfortunate how small towns in the US, especially the rust belt, seem to be wiped out. Everything is closed aside from Dollar Generals and Family Dollars. Biked from Logansport, IN to Chicago Heights, IL, right outside Chicago. 961 miles total. Some left knee pain but otherwise good. Rain should die out tomorrow but I might have to get the bike fixed. Staying with some family in the Chicago area. 👍

caption.text: Day 8 complete. 118 miles today totaling 843. Richmond, IA to Logansport, IN. Got started just before 8 and finished around 6:30. Not much video because not much changed. Cornfields and bike paths surrounded by foliage along the Cardinal Greenway. Sunny and windy. Met a guy running for IN state rep and biked with him for a few miles and I learned about how the BP gas station in Gaston, IA has the best “breaded tenderloin” (I sadly didn’t try). Rain from Beryl coming tomorrow. Resting heart rate has been in the high 30s which is low for me. Saw some pretty crazy things (e.g. one road Google maps sent me down was called “Coon Hunter rd”). Hopefully I can beat much of the rain tmr. 👍

caption.text: Day 7 complete. Said goodbye to parents and Theo the dog in the am then got dropped off and hit the road. 100 solid miles in the heat and sun from Lilly Chapel, OH to Richmond, IA. 725 done so far. Legs were sore and wrist a bit numb, saw lots of cardinals and blue jays, passed innumerable cornfields, saw a raceway with formula one cars, and met a 77 year old guy who had biked across the country 16 times. Hopefully hurricane Beryl doesn’t hit me 👍

caption.text: Day 6 Complete. 125 miles today from Millersburg, Ohio to Lilly Chapel, OH. Flat terrain today so got to hammer out a lot of millage. Went from heavy Amish country to Columbus suburbs to Columbus to farm land. Lots of bike trails in Ohio. Parents and dog driving out to Montana and picked me up at the end of my ride and brought me back to Columbus for the night. On a side note, statistic I looked up says that there are nearly 378k Amish people in the US, had no idea. 👍

caption.text: Day 5 complete. Happy Friday. Pretty glum day today with light drizzles occasionally. Very hilly terrain in central Ohio. Saw lots of farms, a hard hat memorial, many Amish people with a lot of them riding e-bikes around me while dusting me, saw a lady arguing with another with a pistol in her hand, and saw a sign that said “no trespassing, or do I need to speek 12-gauge”. In Millersburg, OH for the night (91 miles outside Columbus) 👍

caption.text: Day 4 complete**. Happy Fourth 🇺🇸. Woke up with aches and a bit of a sore throat but dialed in. Went through many tunnels, went up some steep hills and down others, and saw many deer. In Steubenville, Ohio for the night 👍

caption.text: Day 3 complete. Spending the night in Polypolis, PA around 42 miles south of Pittsburg. Had breakfast with four bikers from New Zealand, biked up 3,000 feet of incline, had lunch in Ohiopyle, and saw too many wind turbines to count 👍

caption.text:
The Route

On July 1st, I am beginning my coast-to-coast bike ride. My planned route is from DC to LA, which is around 3,100 miles. Traveling against the prevailing west-to-east winds, this should take me around 36 days, accounting for some mishaps along the way while aiming to ride 100 miles per day.

From DC to the Nebraska/Wyoming border, I will ride and camp along the Great American Rail Trail, which travels on bike routes by railroad tracks through the country from DC to Western Washington. My planned route takes me through Nevada and Southern California in late July. If I determine the temperature to be too hot, I will switch to following the Great American Rail Trail for the entirety of the trip. This alternate route is around 3,800 miles and should take me 39 days to complete.
